preferences were first added, we originally did not have any preference merging across load path [0].  We later added that [1], but retained the requirement that for each individual element in the load path, preferences must have an entry in `Project.toml` listing the relevant package.  This was partly on purpose (immediately binding the name to the UUID prevents confusion when a UUID<->name binding is not readily apparent) and partly just inheriting the way things worked back when preferences was written with just a single Project.toml in mind.

This PR breaks this assumption to specifically allow an entry in the Julia load path that contains only a single `LocalPreferences.toml` file that sets preferences for packages that may or may not be in the current environment stack.  The usecase and desire for this is well-motivated in [2], but basically boils down to "system admin provided preferences that should be used globally".  In fact, one such convenient method that now works is to drop a `LocalPreferences.toml` file in the `stdlib` directory of your Julia distribution, as that is almost always a part of a Julia process's load path.

One could potentially do something like make a special `_uuid` top-level preference for a package, but I think that's a little ugly and the lookup should just be done in the corresponding Manifest, as for this preference to have an effect at all, the package must be in the manifest.  We do not yet have widespread name pollution from competing registries, we mostly have one big registry (`General`) and smaller, private registries that typically don't shadow names from General.  So in practice, the issues being guarded against here do not yet exist.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2510570125/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

{"repository_id":82341193,"push_id":21051538093,"size":1,"distinct_size":1,"ref":"refs/heads/sf/windows_executable_bit","head":"d7ba620fd18384cca282b4fd241f73d5546ce16a","before":"8f3bb7ae54af7d7368b72ffc9972af35f4b3269e","commits":[{"sha":"d7ba620fd18384cca282b4fd241f73d5546ce16a","author":{"email":"7656a2eee953082d19c97fac033084c74829e1a4@gmail.com","name":"Elliot Saba"},"message":"Fix artifact directories not having traversal permissions

It turns out that Windows requires the executable bit set if the
`BYPASS_TRAVERSE_CHECKING` privilege is not attached to a user's
account.  It's simply more correct to ensure that our directories have
this bit set, and unfortunately our `filemode()` function call is
not complete on Windows and does not include this bit, so we manually
add it in on Windows.","distinct":true,"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/Pkg.jl/commits/d7ba620fd18384cca282b4fd241f73d5546ce16a"}]}

We later added that [1], but retained the requirement that for each individual element in the load path, preferences must have an entry in `Project.toml` listing the relevant package.  This was partly on purpose (immediately binding the name to the UUID prevents confusion when a UUID<->name binding is not readily apparent) and partly just inheriting the way things worked back when preferences was written with just a single Project.toml in mind.

This PR breaks this assumption to specifically allow an entry in the Julia load path that contains only a single `LocalPreferences.toml` file that sets preferences for packages that may or may not be in the current environment stack.  The usecase and desire for this is well-motivated in [2], but basically boils down to "system admin provided preferences that should be used globally".  In fact, one such convenient method that now works is to drop a `LocalPreferences.toml` file in the `stdlib` directory of your Julia distribution, as that is almost always a part of a Julia process's load path.

preferences were first added, we originally did not have any preference merging across load path [0].  We later added that [1], but retained the requirement that for each individual element in the load path, preferences must have an entry in `Project.toml` listing the relevant package.  This was partly on purpose (immediately binding the name to the UUID prevents confusion when a UUID<->name binding is not readily apparent) and partly just inheriting the way things worked back when preferences was written with just a single Project.toml in mind.

This PR breaks this assumption to specifically allow an entry in the Julia load path that contains only a single `LocalPreferences.toml` file that sets preferences for packages that may or may not be in the current environment stack.  The usecase and desire for this is well-motivated in [2], but basically boils down to "system admin provided preferences that should be used globally".  In fact, one such convenient method that now works is to drop a `LocalPreferences.toml` file in the `stdlib` directory of your Julia distribution, as that is almost always a part of a Julia process's load path.
